result.learned:
Benchmark/ladder landscape as of July 2026, per lane:

KAGGLE — Run by Google's Kaggle. Official API/CLI (`pip install kaggle`, new repo Kaggle/kaggle-cli, Apache-2.0 client) authenticates via kaggle.json API token and supports competitions list/files/download/submit/submissions and leaderboard view (`-s`) or full CSV download (`-d`). Live competitions are a poor engine lane: per-competition rules, daily submission caps, website rules-acceptance, and non-deterministic relative scoring (your score depends on a hidden test set + other entrants). The proven adapter shape is OpenAI's MLE-bench: mirrors 75 Kaggle competitions with rebuilt train/test splits and fully LOCAL grading (`mlebench grade`), Docker base image, lite 22-comp subset (158GB) — but recommended compute is 36 vCPU/440GB RAM/24GB GPU per 24h run, far beyond a user laptop. Kaggle ToS page is JS-rendered and could not be verified for republishing terms (open question). Notably ARC Prize 2026 is itself hosted on Kaggle.

HUGGINGFACE — HF Inc. runs the Hub. The Open LLM Leaderboard is officially retired ("completed, retired" per HF staff post); the leaderboard story is now 200+ community leaderboards as Spaces, each backed by its own dataset/license. HF is not an executable benchmark — it is the DISTRIBUTION SUBSTRATE for the others: terminal-bench-2.0 (apache-2.0 dataset), ScaleAI/SWE-bench_Pro, and terminal-bench-2-leaderboard results all live there. Programmatic access: huggingface_hub lib + documented REST Hub API; anonymous works for public repos, token for gated; rate limits are per-bucket with 429+RateLimit-header retry semantics, higher quotas on PRO/Team. Mirroring legality is per-repo license metadata — many leaderboard result datasets are Apache-2.0, so snapshotting is clean where the license says so.

ARC-AGI-3 — ARC Prize Foundation; launched March 25, 2026 as the first fully interactive ARC benchmark: 6 turn-based game environments (3 public: ft09/ls20/vc33; 3 private for final rankings: sp80/lp85/as66). Frontier AI scored <1% (0.51%) at launch vs 100% human. Access: `pip install arc-agi` toolkit — game engine runs LOCALLY at ~2,000 FPS with NO API key and no rate limit (ideal for a Weco/meta-harness inner loop), while the hosted API (free during research preview, ARC_API_KEY, 600 RPM) is required for official scorecards, replays, and leaderboard participation. Scoring is fully specified: Relative Human Action Efficiency — level_score = (human_baseline_actions / ai_actions)^2 capped at 1.15x, weighted per-game aggregation requiring full completion for 100%, overall = mean of game scores; only environment-affecting actions count (reasoning/tool calls free). ARC Prize 2026 Kaggle competition: $700K for first 100% agent, no internet during eval, open-source required, closes Nov 2. Game/data licensing not found in docs (open question).

SWE-BENCH PRO — Scale AI (paper arXiv:2509.16941). Three tiers: public set 731 instances from 11 repos deliberately chosen for strong copyleft (GPL) licenses as contamination deterrent; held-out set (12 repos); commercial set (18 startup codebases, private). ~1,865 tasks total across 41 repos in Python/Go/TS/JS. Harness: github.com/scaleapi/SWE-bench_Pro-os (MIT), dataset at HF ScaleAI/SWE-bench_Pro (58K downloads/mo; no explicit license field found on card — open question). Local run: Docker (or Modal) via `--use_local_docker`; flow = generate patches with your agent → gather_patches.py → swe_bench_pro_eval.py against fail2pass tests. Deterministic test-based scoring, but a May 2026 Datacurve audit reportedly found ~1/3 of trials misgraded (secondary source), and scores fragment across scaffolds (Scale SEAL standardized vs vendor-scaffold numbers differ by 10-20pts). Leaderboards run by Scale's SEAL team; no verified self-serve submission path.

TERMINAL-BENCH 2.0 — tbench.ai / harbor-framework org (Merrill, Shaw, Carlini + 99 contributors; Laude/Stanford lineage). 89 curated hard terminal tasks (count from multiple secondary sources; primary pages don't expose it in fetch), each = English instruction + unique Docker environment + human oracle solution + verification test script; several hours of human+LM validation per task. Everything Apache-2.0 (harness repo, task repo, HF dataset). Official harness is Harbor (`uv tool install harbor`; `harbor run -d terminal-bench/terminal-bench-2 -a claude-code -m anthropic/...`), Docker required, oracle-agent smoke test built in, custom agents pluggable via `--agent-import-path "module:Class"` — exactly the injection point a user's self-evolving agent needs. Binary pass/fail per task → accuracy ± stderr on the tbench.ai leaderboard; submissions per instructions in HF harborframework/terminal-bench-2-leaderboard with team verification (docs also say "new submission process coming soon"). Harbor roadmap: adapters for MLE-Bench, SWE-Lancer, RE-Bench — Harbor is converging on being a multi-benchmark harness itself.

WEBARENA — CMU (Zhou et al. 2023), Apache-2.0, 812 tasks over self-hosted Docker replicas (shopping, CMS admin, Reddit-like forum, GitLab, wiki, map); execution-based functional validators; "official" leaderboard is a Google Sheet, effectively community-maintained now (repo points to AgentLab for modern infra). The 2026-relevant development is ServiceNow's webarena-verified (Apache-2.0, Feb 2, 2026): every task/answer/evaluator manually re-verified, LLM-judging replaced with deterministic type-aware structural comparison, a 258-task Hard subset, Docker images up to 92% smaller with auto-login (the original GitLab image alone was 100GB+), install via uvx/pip/docker, and OFFLINE evaluation via network-trace replay. Tractable now, but still the heaviest environment stack of the six and browser-agent-specific.

RECOMMENDATION (v1 run-adapters): (1) Terminal-Bench 2.0 — first adapter: highest agentic-coding relevance for a Claude-Code-driven loop, best local tractability (Docker+Harbor, 89 tasks, oracle smoke test), deterministic test-script scoring, strong community pull, fully Apache-2.0, and a documented custom-agent injection point; wrapping Harbor may yield MLE-Bench/SWE-Lancer lanes for free later. (2) SWE-Bench Pro public set (sampled subsets) — second adapter: the canonical hard agentic-coding ladder with deterministic fail2pass scoring and MIT harness; run stratified subsets locally to control cost (subsetting is fine because the engine's product is the user's own Pareto frontier, not leaderboard parity); flag grader-quality caveat. (3) ARC-AGI-3 — third adapter (cheap to add): free unlimited LOCAL engine is the single most Weco-loop-friendly substrate of all six (thousands of eval episodes/hour at zero cost), RHAE gives a native action-efficiency axis for the Pareto frontier, and ARC Prize 2026 supplies the strongest community pull; it also forces the adapter interface to support the interactive/stepped variant, future-proofing for WebArena in v2. Kaggle: market/link-out only in v1 (live-submission constraints + unverified ToS; MLE-bench-style offline mirror is a possible later lane but its compute floor exceeds user machines). HuggingFace: not an execution lane — it is the dataset-distribution and leaderboard-snapshot layer inside the other adapters. WebArena: defer to v2 via webarena-verified.

evidence.note: ARC-AGI-3 launched 2026-03-25 by ARC Prize Foundation; interactive turn-based environments; frontier AI <1% vs humans 100%; paper submitted 2026-03-24
pip package runs game engine locally at ~2,000 FPS with no API key and no rate limit; hosted API required for official scorecards/replays/leaderboard

RHAE scoring: level_score=(human_baseline_actions/ai_actions)^2 capped 1.15x; upper-median first-time-player baseline; weighted per-game aggregation; overall = mean of game scores; non-environment actions not counted
hosted API: 600 RPM, free during research preview, no SLA; rate increases via team@arcprize.org
ARC Prize 2026 ARC-AGI-3 track on Kaggle: $700K for first 100%, no internet during eval, open-source required, closes Nov 2; 3 public games (ft09,ls20,vc33) + 3 private (sp80,lp85,as66)
SWE-Bench Pro: public set 731 instances from 11 strong-copyleft (GPL) repos as contamination deterrent; held-out 12 repos; commercial 18 startup codebases; Python/Go/TS/JS
MIT-licensed harness; Docker or Modal execution (--use_local_docker); gather_patches.py → swe_bench_pro_eval.py; fail2pass test scoring; dataset at HF ScaleAI/SWE-bench_Pro
731 rows, single test split; repos incl. NodeBB, qutebrowser, Ansible, Open Library, Teleport, Navidrome; no explicit license field on card; 58K downloads/mo
Scale SEAL runs the public/private/commercial leaderboards; GPT-5.4 (xHigh) tops standardized public set at 59.1% per aggregators

same model yields sharply different SWE-bench Pro scores across vendor scaffold vs SEAL harness vs commercial subset; Datacurve May-2026 audit reportedly found ~1/3 trials misgraded — secondary, unverified

Harbor is the official TB2 harness: uv tool install harbor; harbor run -d terminal-bench/terminal-bench-2 -a claude-code|oracle -m <model>; Docker required; custom agents via --agent-import-path; Apache-2.0; leaderboard submission 'coming soon' per docs, currently via HF harborframework/terminal-bench-2-leaderboard instructions with team verification

TB2 task dataset and task repo both Apache-2.0; each task = instruction + unique env + oracle solution + verification tests; several hours human+LM validation per task; 89-task count from secondary sources (tbench.ai/explainx summaries)
leaderboard shows accuracy ±stderr; ~38 models evaluated; TB team verifies runs; Harbor roadmap includes MLE-Bench/SWE-Lancer/RE-Bench adapters
CMU WebArena: Apache-2.0, 812 tasks, self-hosted Docker sites, execution-based validators, leaderboard = shared Google Sheet, repo defers modern infra to AgentLab

webarena-verified (Apache-2.0, Feb 2026): all 812 tasks manually re-verified, deterministic type-aware evaluators replace LLM judging, 258-task Hard subset, Docker images up to 92% smaller w/ auto-login, uvx/pip/docker install, offline eval via network-trace replay

official Kaggle CLI (Apache-2.0): kaggle.json token auth; competitions list/files/download/submit/submissions; leaderboard view (-s) or full CSV download (-d); code competitions submit by kernel -k/-v

MLE-bench: 75 mirrored Kaggle competitions with rebuilt train/test splits and local grading (mlebench grade); lite=22 comps/158GB; recommended 36 vCPU/440GB RAM/24GB GPU per 24h run — the offline-Kaggle adapter precedent, but heavy
Open LLM Leaderboard officially retired; 200+ community leaderboards now live as HF Spaces with per-repo licenses
HF Hub REST API + huggingface_hub lib; bucketed rate limits, 429 + RateLimit header auto-retry in hub lib >=1.2.0, higher quotas on PRO/Team; per-repo license metadata governs reuse

next: Feed this brief into M1 design synthesis: draft the RunAdapter interface (TaskSource/Sandbox/AgentBinding/Scorer/Submitter) in DESIGN.md with TB2, SWE-Bench Pro (sampled), ARC-AGI-3 as v1 lanes

Prototype spike: `uv tool install harbor` + oracle smoke run (harbor run -d terminal-bench/terminal-bench-2 -a oracle -l 5) to measure real wall-time/disk on a user-class machine before committing the adapter contract

Verify open questions before the market page ships: Kaggle ToS/Meta-Kaggle license via authenticated browser session (JS-rendered pages blocked fetch), SWE-bench_Pro HF dataset license field via Scale contact or repo LICENSE file, ARC-AGI-3 game/toolkit license via pip package metadata (pip show arc-agi / PyPI page)
Confirm TB2 leaderboard submission mechanics + license of harborframework/terminal-bench-2-leaderboard dataset (determines whether the market page can mirror TB2 leaderboard rows verbatim)

Cross-check with the Weco lane: ARC-AGI-3 local engine (free, 2K FPS) is the cheapest inner-loop eval signal — validate Weco's evaluate-command contract can wrap `arc-agi` local episodes and `harbor run` exit artifacts
key_facts: ARC-AGI-3 launched 2026-03-25 (ARC Prize Foundation): 6 games (3 public ft09/ls20/vc33, 3 private), frontier AI 0.51% vs human 100% at launch
ARC-AGI-3 local engine via `pip install arc-agi` runs ~2,000 FPS locally, no API key, no rate limit; hosted API (free research preview, 600 RPM) required only for official scorecards/leaderboard
ARC-AGI-3 scoring = Relative Human Action Efficiency: (human_baseline/ai_actions)^2 per level, capped 1.15x, weighted per-game, overall = mean of games

SWE-Bench Pro (Scale AI, arXiv:2509.16941): public 731 instances/11 GPL repos (copyleft as contamination deterrent) + held-out 12 repos + commercial 18 startup codebases; MIT harness scaleapi/SWE-bench_Pro-os; Docker local eval via --use_local_docker; fail2pass test scoring
SWE-Bench Pro scores fragment by scaffold (vendor vs Scale SEAL standardized differ ~10-20pts); May-2026 audit claims ~1/3 trials misgraded (secondary source)
Terminal-Bench 2.0: 89 hard terminal tasks (secondary-source count), each = instruction + unique Docker env + oracle solution + test script; task repo, dataset, and Harbor harness all Apache-2.0

TB2 official harness = Harbor: `uv tool install harbor`; `harbor run -d terminal-bench/terminal-bench-2 -a claude-code -m anthropic/...`; custom agents via --agent-import-path; oracle agent = built-in smoke test; roadmap adds MLE-Bench/SWE-Lancer/RE-Bench adapters

WebArena (CMU, Apache-2.0): 812 tasks on self-hosted Docker sites; ServiceNow webarena-verified (Feb 2026, Apache-2.0) re-verified all tasks, deterministic evaluators, 258-task Hard subset, Docker images up to 92% smaller, offline eval via network-trace replay; leaderboard is an unmaintained Google Sheet

Kaggle official CLI (Apache-2.0 client, kaggle.json token): competitions download/submit/submissions + full leaderboard CSV download; live comps have daily submission caps + per-comp rules; MLE-bench mirrors 75 Kaggle comps for local grading but recommends 36 vCPU/440GB RAM/24GB GPU

HF Open LLM Leaderboard is retired; leaderboards are now 200+ community Spaces; HF Hub is the dataset-distribution layer for TB2 and SWE-Bench Pro; access via huggingface_hub + REST API, bucketed rate limits, per-repo license metadata
ARC Prize 2026 ARC-AGI-3 competition runs ON Kaggle: $700K for first 100%, no-internet eval, open-source required, closes Nov 2
design_implications: Adapter interface needs FIVE slots the six ladders jointly imply: TaskSource (pinned dataset id+version, HF-first: TB2 and SWE-P both distribute via HF Hub → one shared fetch/cache layer), Sandbox (Docker container per task is the universal substrate — TB2, SWE-P, WebArena, MLE-bench all assume it), AgentBinding (the user-agent injection point), Scorer (deterministic verifier returning per-task pass/fail PLUS cost axes), Submitter (optional, per-ladder, mostly absent — agentdex's own ledger is the primary record)

Sandbox must support TWO execution shapes: batch-episode (agent works inside container, tests run after — TB2, SWE-Bench Pro) and interactive-stepped (action/observation loop against an env API — ARC-AGI-3 now, WebArena v2). Designing both in v1 via ARC-AGI-3 future-proofs the interface cheaply

AgentBinding precedents to copy: Harbor's --agent-import-path 'module:Class' (TB2), patch-emitting harness + gather_patches.py (SWE-P), env-loop agent over arc-agi SDK (ARC). The user's self-evolving Claude-Code agent must be expressible in all three — argues for a thin agentdex-agent shim per lane rather than one universal agent API

Pareto axes fall out naturally: task score vs $ cost vs wall-time vs tokens vs ACTIONS — ARC-AGI-3's RHAE is literally an action-efficiency metric, i.e., one ladder already scores ON a Pareto axis; the engine should record actions/tokens/wall-time/spend per episode uniformly across adapters

Subset sampling must be first-class in the measurement contract: SWE-P 731 tasks is too heavy for a laptop full-run; TB2's 89 is a comfortable full-run; webarena-verified ships an official 258-task Hard subset — precedent for named, pinned subsets. A user's Pareto point must carry (dataset, version, subset, n) provenance to be comparable

Version pinning is mandatory: Harbor's -d dataset-name@version pattern and webarena-verified's version-controlled task releases are the models; unpinned runs make Pareto frontiers incomparable across engine versions

BYO-creds maps cleanly: TB2/SWE-P need the user's own model API key (or Claude Code subscription via claude-code agent), ARC-AGI-3 online needs a FREE ARC_API_KEY (local needs none), Kaggle needs user's kaggle.json — no lane requires agentdex to hold credentials, consistent with the locus constraint

Market page legal split — CAN mirror: TB2 tasks/leaderboard-adjacent artifacts (Apache-2.0 repo+dataset; confirm leaderboard-results dataset license), WebArena + webarena-verified task metadata (Apache-2.0), ARC-AGI-3 public-game metadata and published human-baseline facts (cite; license open question), HF dataset metadata via Hub API (per-repo license). MUST link-out/snapshot-with-citation only: Kaggle competition pages + live leaderboards (ToS unverified; use official API leaderboard CSV only for the user's OWN account context), Scale SEAL leaderboard pages (proprietary presentation; individual scores are facts but page content is Scale's), HF community leaderboard Spaces (per-Space license)

Kaggle's role in v1 is market-only, and that's still valuable: ARC Prize 2026 lives on Kaggle, so the market page's ARC entry naturally deep-links to a Kaggle competition — one card can carry both ladder metadata and competition/prize framing

The inner Weco loop wants a cheap, fast, deterministic eval signal: ARC-AGI-3 local engine (free, ~2K FPS, no rate limit) and TB2 single-task runs (--include-task-name) are the two candidates; SWE-P is outer-loop/checkpoint-eval only due to cost. Adapter interface should expose a 'cheap-probe' tier (subset or single-task) distinct from 'full-measurement' tier

Harbor is a build-vs-wrap decision: it is becoming a general multi-benchmark harness (MLE-Bench/SWE-Lancer/RE-Bench adapters on roadmap). Wrapping Harbor as agentdex's execution backend for the batch-episode shape could deliver several future ladders for free, at the cost of a dependency on their release cadence — flag for the architecture doc

Grader-quality risk (SWE-P ~1/3 misgrade claim, WebArena's history of broken validators fixed only by webarena-verified) argues the engine should store raw per-task artifacts (patches, traces, test output), not just scores, so frontiers can be re-scored when harnesses fix graders — matches 'measurement is the moat'
open_questions: Kaggle ToS/AUP exact terms on republishing competition metadata + leaderboards on a third-party market page — kaggle.com/terms and the Meta Kaggle dataset license are JS-rendered and could not be fetched; verify via authenticated browser before mirroring anything Kaggle
License of the HF ScaleAI/SWE-bench_Pro dataset itself (card shows no license field; underlying repos are GPL — does GPL propagate to the task JSON? affects redistribution inside agentdex caches)

ARC-AGI-3 licensing of the games/toolkit (docs index has no licensing page; check PyPI arc-agi package license metadata) and whether arcprize.org terms restrict republishing scorecard/leaderboard data
Whether the 3 ARC-AGI-3 PRIVATE games are reachable only through the Kaggle competition or also via the hosted API with a registered key

TB2 exact task count from a primary source (89 is consistently reported by secondary sources; primary pages didn't expose the number in fetches) and the license/mechanics of the harborframework/terminal-bench-2-leaderboard submission dataset
SWE-Bench Pro official self-serve leaderboard submission path (none found — SEAL appears to run evals themselves); also whether Scale permits third-party republication of SEAL leaderboard numbers

Real disk/RAM/wall-time footprint of a full webarena-verified stack and of SWE-Bench Pro per-instance images on a typical user machine (92%-smaller and Docker-based respectively, but no primary numbers found) — needs the M2 prototype spike, not more reading
Whether Datacurve's ~1/3 misgrading claim against SWE-bench Pro graders holds up (secondary source only; if true it strengthens the store-raw-artifacts design decision)

The 'hosted API on Anthropic's servers' phrasing in one fetched summary of ARC docs is likely a summarizer artifact (ARC Prize runs its own API) — verify infrastructure owner if it ever matters for latency/ToS
